questions.question: What happens when an exception is thrown inside a try block and a matching catch block exists?
options: The remaining code in the try block is skipped and execution jumps to the matching catch block
The remaining code in the try block finishes before the catch block runs
The catch block runs and then the try block resumes where it left off
questions.answer: The remaining code in the try block is skipped and execution jumps to the matching catch block

questions.question: Which exception is thrown when you attempt to divide an integer by zero?
questions.answer: ArithmeticException
A C D

questions.question: Which of the following will cause a NullPointerException?
questions.answer: String s = null; s.length();

questions.question: When does the finally block execute?
options: Only when an exception is thrown
Only when no exception is thrown
Whether or not an exception is thrown
Only when the catch block does not handle the exception
questions.answer: Whether or not an exception is thrown

options: In try, In catch, In finally, Done
In try, In catch, In finally
questions.answer: In try, In catch, In finally, Done

questions.question: What happens if an exception is thrown but the catch block specifies the wrong exception type?
options: The exception is not caught and propagates up the call stack
The finally block catches it instead
questions.answer: The exception is not caught and propagates up the call stack

questions.question: Which line of code would throw a StringIndexOutOfBoundsException?
questions.answer: "hello".charAt(5);

questions.question: What is the correct order for multiple catch blocks?
options: General exception types first, then more specific ones
Specific exception types first, then more general ones
Alphabetical order by exception class name
questions.answer: Specific exception types first, then more general ones

questions.question: What happens if you place catch(Exception e) before catch(ArithmeticException e)?
options: The code runs normally and the more specific catch is used
A compilation error occurs because the more specific catch is unreachable
questions.answer: A compilation error occurs because the more specific catch is unreachable

questions.question: Which method call on an exception object returns a human-readable description of the error?
questions.answer: e.getMessage()

options: In try - no error, In catch, In finally
In try - no error, In finally
questions.answer: In try - no error, In finally

questions.question: Which exception is thrown by Integer.parseInt("abc")?
questions.answer: NumberFormatException

questions.question: What does e.getClass().getSimpleName() return when called on a NullPointerException object?
questions.answer: "NullPointerException"
NullPointerException

questions.question: Which of the following is true about the finally block?
options: It is required whenever a try block is used
It runs only if no exception is thrown
It is commonly used for cleanup tasks like closing files or releasing resources
questions.answer: It is commonly used for cleanup tasks like closing files or releasing resources

questions.question: What exception is thrown when you cast an object to an incompatible type?
questions.answer: ClassCastException

questions.question: What does it mean for an exception to be "thrown" in Java?
options: The program deletes the variable that caused the error
An exception object is created and the normal flow of execution is disrupted
The compiler generates a warning message
The program automatically fixes the error and continues
questions.answer: An exception object is created and the normal flow of execution is disrupted

questions.question: What happens to code after a try-catch block when the exception is successfully caught?
options: It only runs if there is a finally block
questions.answer: It executes normally

questions.question: Which of the following will NOT throw an exception?
questions.answer: Integer.parseInt("42");

questions.question: What is the difference between preventing an error and handling an error?
options: There is no difference; they are the same thing
Preventing checks conditions before risky code; handling uses try-catch to respond after an exception occurs
Preventing uses try-catch; handling uses if-else statements
Preventing stops the program; handling lets it crash gracefully
questions.answer: Preventing checks conditions before risky code; handling uses try-catch to respond after an exception occurs

questions.question: What is the result of dividing a double value by zero in Java?
options: The result is Infinity, and no exception is thrown
questions.answer: The result is Infinity, and no exception is thrown

questions.question: Which approach makes a program "crash-proof" when reading user input for a number?
options: Using Scanner.nextInt() without any error handling
Wrapping Integer.parseInt() in a while loop with try-catch to re-prompt on invalid input
Converting the input to a char array and checking each character manually
Using a finally block to read the number
questions.answer: Wrapping Integer.parseInt() in a while loop with try-catch to re-prompt on invalid input

options: Before try, In try, In catch, After try-catch
Before try, In try, Result: 5, After try-catch
Before try, In try, Result: 5, In catch, After try-catch
Before try, In try, After try-catch
questions.answer: Before try, In try, Result: 5, After try-catch

questions.question: Which of the following exceptions is caused specifically by accessing an invalid index in an array?
questions.answer: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
